plainMeaning: Next I bow to the teacher Bhūtadinna, who never grew weary in penance and self-control and was honoured by the wise as a true monk and a master of monastic discipline.
recitation: tatto a bhūyadinnaṃ, niccaṃ tava saṃjame anivviṇaṃ; paṇḍia-jaṇa-sāmaṇṇaṃ, vandāmi ya saṃyama-vihinnū.

wordByWord.gloss: thereafter, after that one, next (in the succession)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Skt. tataḥ (ablative of tad, 'from that'); the chain-link adverb that hands the homage from the previous teacher to this one.
wordByWord.notes: Carries the 'and-then' momentum of the paṭṭāvalī from verse to verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit reduced form of Skt. ca ('and'); commonly written 'a'/'ya' in these gāthās.
wordByWord.notes: Connective joining this name to the foregoing roll of elders.

wordByWord.gloss: Bhūtadinna (the ācārya) — accusative, the object of 'I bow'
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. Bhūtadinna / Bhūtadatta: bhūya (< bhūta, 'beings; the real') + dinna (< datta, 'given') — 'one given by/to the spirits' or 'bestowed by the real'; a proper name.
wordByWord.notes: A guardian of the Kālika-śruta anuyoga in the later lineage; the accusative -ṃ marks him as the one venerated.
Governs the whole phrase 'unwearied in tapas and saṃyama'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Skt. tapas; here in compound/locative force, 'tava(-saṃjame)' = 'in austerity (and restraint)'.
wordByWord.notes: Paired with saṃjama as the twin Jain disciplines.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit saṃjama < Skt. saṃyama (saṃ + √yam, 'to hold together, restrain'); locative singular -e, 'in restraint'.
wordByWord.notes: Locative locates the unwearying (anivviṇaṃ) in both tapas and saṃyama.

wordByWord.gloss: untiring, never flagging, never disgusted or disheartened
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Skt. a-nirviṇṇa (a- privative + nirviṇṇa, 'wearied, repelled, despondent'); accusative agreeing with Bhūtadinna.
wordByWord.notes: Litotes: not merely 'diligent' but 'never once flagging' — the antidote to nirveda when bearing parīṣahas.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Skt. paṇḍita ('learned'); first member of the compound paṇḍia-jaṇa.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies jaṇa ('people') — the discerning, scripturally trained.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jaṇa < Skt. jana ('person, people'); compounded as paṇḍia-jaṇa, 'the learned folk'.
wordByWord.notes: The audience whose esteem certifies his śramaṇahood.

wordByWord.gloss: (true) monkhood, śramaṇahood; one held a genuine śramaṇa (among the wise)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Skt. śrāmaṇya ('the state of a śramaṇa, monkhood'); accusative. (A homophone sāmaṇṇa < sāmānya, 'common, general', also exists — see notes.)
wordByWord.notes: Read as śrāmaṇya: 'paṇḍia-jaṇa-sāmaṇṇaṃ' = 'whose monkhood is acknowledged by the learned'.

wordByWord.gloss: I bow, I venerate, I make homage
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vandāmi < Skt. vande (√vand, 'to praise, salute'); 1st person singular present.
wordByWord.notes: The hymn's governing act, here applied to Bhūtadinna.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ya < Skt. ca ('and'); enclitic connective.
wordByWord.notes: Couples 'I bow' to the final epithet saṃyama-vihinnū.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. saṃyama (saṃ + √yam); first member of the compound saṃyama-vihinnū.
wordByWord.notes: Here the object-domain of his expertise: the rules of restraint.

wordByWord.gloss: knower of the method/discipline (of restraint); versed in the rules
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vihi-nnū < Skt. vidhi-jña (vidhi, 'rule, procedure' + jña, 'knower'); accusative agreeing with Bhūtadinna.
wordByWord.notes: Praise-context favours vidhi-jña ('rule-knower'); a homophone vihinna (< vidhvasta/vihīna, 'disturbed/bereft') is possible but contextually rejected — see notes.

literalTranslation: And thereafter (I bow to) Bhūtadinna, ever unwearied in austerity and self-restraint; held a true śramaṇa among the learned — and I bow to him, the knower of the discipline of self-restraint.
idiomaticTranslation: And then to Bhūtadinna I bow: a teacher who, in penance and self-mastery, never once flagged or wearied; the discerning hailed him a true monk, and a sure master of the whole discipline of restraint — to him I make my homage.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having just bowed to Govinda and the keepers of the Kālika-śruta exposition, the hymn now hands the homage onward — 'tatto a', 'and next' — to Bhūtadinna. The verse paints him with three strokes. First, his inner labour: 'niccaṃ tava saṃjame anivviṇaṃ', constantly unwearied in austerity and restraint. The chosen word is a litotes — not 'energetic' but 'never disgusted, never flagging' (a-nirviṇṇa) — which is the harder praise, for it claims he never once slackened under the long grind of ascetic life. Second, his public standing: 'paṇḍia-jaṇa-sāmaṇṇaṃ', his śramaṇahood was vouched for not by the crowd but by the learned, the very people equipped to judge a monk's authenticity. Third, his mastery of the rule: 'saṃyama-vihinnū', a knower of the vidhi of restraint — fitting for a transmitter whose task was to guard not only the letter of the anuyoga but the lived discipline behind it. The three epithets climb from interior endurance, to outward recognition, to expert command, so the single act 'vandāmi' lands on a teacher complete in practice, repute, and knowledge — and the chain is ready to pass to the gold-and-lotus master named next.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain pairing of tapas and saṃyama — the twin engines of saṃvara (stopping inflow) and nirjarā (shedding karma). 'Anivviṇaṃ' names the precise virtue that keeps that engine running: the refusal of nirveda (weariness/aversion) when enduring parīṣahas, so that austerity does not curdle into despondency. 'Saṃyama-vihinnū' points to saṃyama as a codified discipline — traditionally counted in seventeen kinds — that one does not merely practise but must know correctly to transmit; in a lineage hymn this knowledge of the rule is itself the credential of a reliable link in the chain.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For today's reciter the verse sets a quiet standard: consistency over intensity. Bhūtadinna's distinction is not a single heroic feat but never tiring — the steady, unspectacular continuance of practice. Bow here as a reminder that authenticity is confirmed by the discerning, and that restraint is a discipline to be learned rightly, not merely felt.

crossReferences.text: Daśavaikālika 1.1 — 'dhammo maṅgalam ukkiṭṭhaṃ, ahiṃsā saṃjamo tavo' (the pairing of saṃyama and tapas as supreme dharma)
Uttarādhyayana ch।2 (Parīṣaha) — bearing hardships without nirveda, the virtue named here as anivviṇa
Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.2–9.6 — saṃvara and nirjarā through gupti, samiti, tapas (doctrinal frame for tava-saṃjama)

sources.title: Nandī Sūtra (Nandisuttam) — Prakrit text, Sthavirāvalī section
sources.consultedFor: The Nandī Sūtra Sthavirāvalī sequence and the placement of Bhūtadinna among the later Kālika-śruta anuyoga teachers.

sources.title: Illustrated Shri Nandi Sutra
sources.consultedFor: Devanagari/Prakrit text and gloss of the teacher-lineage gāthās naming Bhūtadinna and the surrounding sthaviras.

sources.title: Nandi Sutra (Umaravkunwar ed.) — scanned canonical text
sources.consultedFor: Verification of the Sthavirāvalī wording and the names adjacent to Bhūtadinna (Govinda, Lohitya, Dūṣyagaṇi).

sources.title: Nandisuttam: A Prakrit Jaina Canonical Text — Text with Sanskrit Rendering, Variant Readings, English Translation and Appendices
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit equivalents (Bhūtadinna < Bhūtadatta, anivviṇaṃ < anirviṇṇa, vihinnū < vidhijña) and variant readings of the lineage gāthās.

notes: The recited text follows the Gujarati source verbatim (e.g. ભૂયદિન્નં, અનિવ્વિણં, વિહિન્નૂ) and is not normalized. Two tokens carry ambiguity: (1) સામણ્ણં is read as śrāmaṇya ('monkhood'), giving 'his monkhood is vouched for by the learned'; a homophone sāmaṇṇa < sāmānya ('common/general esteem') would yield 'generally esteemed among the learned' — sense is close either way. (2) વિહિન્નૂ is taken as vidhi-jña ('knower of the rule of restraint'), strongly favoured by the laudatory context; the homophonous vihinna (< vidhvasta/vihīna, 'disturbed, bereft') is grammatically possible but contradicts a praise verse and is rejected. Name-equation Bhūyadinna = Bhūtadinna/Bhūtadatta and his exact dates are traditional rather than firmly historical. The gold-tempered/spotless-lotus epithets of the following verse may extend to Bhūtadinna or attach to the next-named teacher; the source punctuation treats them as a fresh figure.
